A low pressure system that developed over the Southern Plains brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to northwest New Jersey. The worst reported conditions were over the higher terrain of Sussex County where temperatures failed to get above the freezing mark. Precipitation began as snow in all areas between 9 a.m. and 11 a.m. EST. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain by Noon EST and changed over to plain rain during the early afternoon in Warren and Morris Counties and central and southern Sussex County. Precipitation remained freezing rain over the northern and higher terrain locations in Sussex County until it ended during the evening. In Morris and Warren Counties there was little, if any, snow accumulation and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. Across Sussex County, accumulations reached up to 2 inches and ice accretions ranged from one-tenth of an inch in the central and southern part of the county to around one-quarter of an inch in the northern part of the county. Many after school and evening activities were cancelled. Weather related accidents with minor injuries occurred in Morris County.Accumulations included 2 inches in Wantage (Sussex County). The winter weather was caused by a low pressure system that moved from eastern Texas on the morning of the second to Michigan on the morning of the 3rd. A secondary low pressure system formed in North Carolina on the morning of the 3rd and moved north and passed close to New York City early in the evening on the 3rd before moving into Maine early in the morning on the 4th. While the high pressure system position at the onset of precipitation was not in an ideal position to lock in the cold air (It was already over Nova Scotia.), the recent unseasonably cold weather kept sufficient cold air in place near the surface for the wintry mix in northwestern New Jersey.

The combination of a low pressure system moving eastnortheast off the North Carolina coast and a high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes produced a northeast flow along coastal New Jersey. This caused increased tides along the shore and some minor tidal flooding in Cape May County around Midnight EST on the 11th. The high tide in Cape May City reached 6.79 feet above mean lower low water at 1130 p.m. EST on the 10th. The low pressure system moved from the Tennessee Valley the morning of March 9th, into Georgia during the late afternoon on the 9th, off the southern North Carolina during the late evening of the 9th and well off the North Carolina coast by the morning of the 10th.

An offshore low pressure system's western edge of its precipitation shield passed over New Jersey and dropped heavy snow from Cape May County north through Ocean County. Accumulations averaged 4 to 6 inches. Farther to the north or west, there was a sharp drop in snowfall intensity and accumulations were generally 2 inches or less. Snow began falling during the late afternoon. Temperatures remained above or near freezing through the evening commute and the snow did not start accumulating on roadways until after the commute was over. The first reports of accumulating snow were received after 8 p.m. EST. The snow fell heavy at times during the second half of the evening and ended during the early morning of the 18th. No major accidents were reported on either the Garden State Parkway or Atlantic City Expressway. Schools were closed on the 18th in and around Atlantic City. Specific accumulations included 6.5 inches in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 6.0 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 5.5 inches in Egg Harbor City (Atlantic County), 5.0 inches in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), Ship Bottom (Ocean County), Green Creek and Cold Spring (Cape May County), 4.5 inches in Woodbine (Cape May County), 4.1 inches in Cape May City (Cape May County), 3.9 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.8 inches in Port Elizabeth (Cumberland County), 3.7 inches in Beachwood (Ocean County), 2.5 inches in Browns Mills (Burlington County) and Manasquan (Monmouth County), 2.3 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 2.0 inches in Oakhurst (Monmouth County) and 0.8 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County). The low pressure system responsible for the snow developed along the coastal waters east of Georgia during the morning of the 17th. By the early evening of the 17th, a pair of low pressure systems were located east of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina and Charleston, South Carolina. These combined into one overnight and passed about 250 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast on the morning of the 18th. The low was located too far offshore to back the heavy snow bands any farther west than the southeastern New Jersey coastal counties.

An offshore low pressure system's western edge of its precipitation shield passed over New Jersey and dropped heavy snow from Cape May County north through Ocean County. Accumulations averaged 4 to 6 inches. Farther to the north or west, there was a sharp drop in snowfall intensity and accumulations were generally 2 inches or less. Snow began falling during the late afternoon. Temperatures remained above or near freezing through the evening commute and the snow did not start accumulating on roadways until after the commute was over. Specific accumulations included 6.5 inches in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 6.0 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 5.5 inches in Egg Harbor City (Atlantic County), 5.0 inches in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), Ship Bottom (Ocean County), Green Creek and Cold Spring (Cape May County), 4.5 inches in Woodbine (Cape May County), 4.1 inches in Cape May City (Cape May County), 3.9 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.8 inches in Port Elizabeth (Cumberland County), 3.7 inches in Beachwood (Ocean County), 2.5 inches in Browns Mills (Burlington County) and Manasquan (Monmouth County), 2.3 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 2.0 inches in Oakhurst (Monmouth County) and 0.8 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County). A low pressure system that developed over the Southern Plains brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to northwest New Jersey. The worst reported conditions were over the higher terrain of Sussex County where temperatures failed to get above the freezing mark. Precipitation began as snow in all areas between 9 a.m. and 11 a.m. EST. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain by Noon EST and changed over to plain rain during the early afternoon in Warren and Morris Counties and central and southern Sussex County. Precipitation remained freezing rain over the northern and higher terrain locations in Sussex County until it ended during the evening. In Morris and Warren Counties there was little, if any, snow accumulation and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. Across Sussex County, accumulations reached up to 2 inches and ice accretions ranged from one-tenth of an inch in the central and southern part of the county to around one-quarter of an inch in the northern part of the county. Many after school and evening activities were cancelled. Weather related accidents with minor injuries occurred in Morris County.Accumulations included 2 inches in Wantage (Sussex County). The winter weather was caused by a low pressure system that moved from eastern Texas on the morning of the second to Michigan on the morning of the 3rd. A secondary low pressure system formed in North Carolina on the morning of the 3rd and moved north and passed close to New York City early in the evening on the 3rd before moving into Maine early in the morning on the 4th. While the high pressure system position at the onset of precipitation was not in an ideal position to lock in the cold air (It was already over Nova Scotia.), the recent unseasonably cold weather kept sufficient cold air in place near the surface for the wintry mix in northwestern New Jersey.

A low pressure system from the Gulf of Mexico brought a winter storm of snow, sleet and freezing rain to northwest New Jersey and a lighter mix of wintry precipitation throughout most of the rest of New Jersey. In northwestern New Jersey, snowfall accumulations averaged 1 to 2 inches, but ice accretions reached around half an inch, especially in Sussex County. Elsewhere in New Jersey, snowfall accumulations were generally less than one inch and ice accretions were less than one quarter of an inch. In the southern half of New Jersey, precipitation began as snow around midnight EST on the 6th. It changed quickly to sleet and freezing rain. Along the immediate coast, precipitation started as and fell as rain throughout the event. As warmer air moved in, precipitation changed to plain rain inland between 4 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST. In the northern half of the state, precipitation started as snow after midnight EST on the 6th. It changed to sleet and freezing rain between 5 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST. The warmer air had a harder time moving northwest and the transition to plain rain which began in Mercer and Monmouth Counties around 8 a.m. EST took until the afternoon in northwest New Jersey and until around 6 p.m. EST in all of Sussex County. Untreated roadways and walkways were very treacherous. Precipitation changed to plain rain around daybreak on the 6th. The low pressure system responsible for the wintry weather developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley.) moved through the region during the night of the 6th. The initial cold air near the surface was provided by a high pressure system from Eastern Canada. It moved offshore overnight on the 5th and during the day on the 6th as the southeast flow around it brought in warmer air from the Atlantic Ocean into the region. But, it took most of the day for the warmer air near the ground to reach northwest New Jersey.

In northwestern New Jersey, snowfall accumulations averaged 1 to 2 inches, but ice accretions reached around half an inch, especially in Sussex County. Elsewhere in New Jersey, snowfall accumulations were generally less than one inch and ice accretions were less than one quarter of an inch. In the southern half of New Jersey, precipitation began as snow around midnight EST on the 6th. It changed quickly to sleet and freezing rain. Along the immediate coast, precipitation started as and fell as rain throughout the event. As warmer air moved in, precipitation changed to plain rain inland between 4 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST. In the northern half of the state, precipitation started as snow after midnight EST on the 6th. It changed to sleet and freezing rain between 5 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST. The warmer air had a harder time moving northwest and the transition to plain rain which began in Mercer and Monmouth Counties around 8 a.m. EST took until the afternoon in northwest New Jersey and until around 6 p.m. EST in all of Sussex County. Untreated roadways and walkways were very treacherous. Precipitation changed to plain rain around daybreak on the 6th. The low pressure system responsible for the wintry weather developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley.) moved through the region during the night of the 6th. The initial cold air near the surface was provided by a high pressure system from Eastern Canada. It moved offshore overnight on the 5th and during the day on the 6th as the southeast flow around it brought in warmer air from the Atlantic Ocean into the region. But, it took most of the day for the warmer air near the ground to reach northwest New Jersey.

A quick moving occluded front and a low pressure system that developed on it off the Delmarva Peninsula dropped a quick one to four inches across the northern half of New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 8th. Some locally higher accumulations occurred in Morris and Sussex Counties. Most of the accumulations were on grassy surfaces except for extreme northwest New Jersey where some slushy snow accumulated on secondary and tertiary roads. Precipitation began as rain around midnight EST on the 8th. As the low pressure system intensified off the Delmarva Peninsula and precipitation fell heavier, the rain changed to snow in most areas between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST. When the precipitation intensity weakened at the end of the event, in some places the snow changed back to rain before ending by noon EST. Specific accumulations included 4.5 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 4.3 inches in Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 4.0 inches in Milton (Morris County), 3.6 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.3 inches in Budd Lake (Morris County) and Clarksburg (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Chatham (Morris County), Blairstown (Warren County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.7 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in South River (Middlesex County), 1.5 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 1.3 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.2 inches in Pottersville and Belle Mead (Somerset County).An occluded front moved from the Upper Mississippi Valley on the evening of the 6th, to the Great Lakes the morning of the 7th, to central Pennsylvania the evening of the 7th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of this occluded front over the Delmarva coastal waters during the early morning of the 8th. It intensified rapidly, but was also moving offshore quickly. Before it moved far enough offshore, it gave the northern half of New Jersey several hours of accumulating snow.

The pressure difference (gradient) between an intensifying low pressure system south of Cape Cod, Massachusetts and a high pressure system over the western Great Lakes produced wind gusts to around 45 mph during the evening of the 4th across eastern New Jersey. The peak wind gusts at both the Atlantic City International and Newark Liberty International Airports were 46 mph.

The combination of heavy rain and snow melt caused poor drainage flooding across central and southern New Jersey. Some creek and river flooding also occurred. The heaviest rain fell from the middle of the morning through the middle of the evening on the 6th. Over southeastern New Jersey most of the heavy rain fell during the afternoon and early evening. In Middlesex County, the combination of the heavy rain and tidal flooding along the Raritan River closed the Landing Lane Bridge between Piscataway and New Brunswick for most of the day on the 6th. Johnson Park was also flooded. Numerous roads were flooded in East Brunswick. In Burlington County, a vehicle became stuck in flood waters in Edgewater Park.  Storm totals included 2.28 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 2.16 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.10 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 2.04 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.96 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.92 inches in Wrightstown (Burlington County) and Petersburg (Cape May County), 1.70 inches in Clinton (Hunterdon County), 1.68 inches in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 1.65 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 1.36 inches in Beach Haven (Ocean County), 1.25 inches in Margate (Atlantic County) and 1.18 inches in Wildwood (Cape May County). The low pressure system responsible for the heavy rain developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley.) moved through the region during the night of the 6th and ended the rain. A slow moving low pressure at both the surface and aloft produced periods of rain across New Jersey from the morning of the 12th into the evening of the 13th. Storm totals included 2.28 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 2.16 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.10 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 2.04 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.96 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.92 inches in Wrightstown (Burlington County) and Petersburg (Cape May County), 1.70 inches in Clinton (Hunterdon County), 1.68 inches in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 1.65 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 1.36 inches in Beach Haven (Ocean County), 1.25 inches in Margate (Atlantic County) and 1.18 inches in Wildwood (Cape May County). The low pressure system responsible for the heavy rain developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley.) moved through the region during the night of the 6th and ended the rain. Precipitation initially began as a wintry mix during the late evening of the 5th before changing to rain during the morning of the 6th in central and southwestern New Jersey.

A late winter storm dropped heavy snow in Northwestern New Jersey, a wintry mix in the Raritan Basin and the rest of the northern half of New Jersey and snow to rain across the eastern Philadelphia suburbs on the 16th. Numerous accidents occurred and traffic was snarled on Interstates 80 and 287 and U.S Routes 1 and 9 in the northern half of the state. Several vehicles overturned on the New Jersey Turnpike and one overturned on Interstate 295 in Burlington County. Most of the accidents were minor and involved vehicles slipping off of roads and into signs and ditches. In the northern half of the state, some schools were closed. Most others dismissed early. Evening classes and government meetings were cancelled. Snow spread from south to north during the morning of the 16th. As warmer air moved in from the Atlantic Ocean, precipitation changed to rain in the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area during the early afternoon of the 16th. Farther north in the Raritan Valley and adjacent parts of northern New Jersey, it took longer for precipitation to change to rain by early evening. Precipitation then changed back to snow later that evening before ending shortly after midnight EST on the 17th. In Northwest New Jersey, precipitation fell mainly as snow, as some sleet occurred during the afternoon and early evening. The snow ended shortly after midnight EST on the 17th. Accumulations ranged from two to eight inches, with the highest amounts in Sussex and Morris Counties.  In Mercer County, serious accidents occurred in five townships. Northbound U.S. Route 1 was closed during the evening in Lawrence Township because of several accidents. In East Windsor Township, a school bus slid into a tree. On the same block, another school bus collided with a car. Nineteen children and two adults suffered cuts and bruises. In Somerset County, Interstate 287 was closed in Bedminster Township because of a serious accident. Several accidents also occurred on Interstate 287 in Bridgewater Township.  Specific accumulations included 7.5 inches in Sparta (Sussex County), 7.0 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County), 6.5 inches in Milton (Morris County), 6.3 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 6.1 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 6.0 inches in Whippany (Morris County), Blairstown (Warren County)  and Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 5.5 inches in Boonton (Morris County) and Newton (Sussex County), 5.0 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), Hackettstown (Warren County) and Metuchen (Middlesex County), 4.5 inches in Manalapan (Monmouth County), 4.3 inches in Somerville (Somerset County), 4.0 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 3.8 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County) and Belle Mead (Somerset County), 3.5 inches in Ewing Township (Mercer County),  New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Manasquan (Monmouth County), 3.1 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.8 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 2.2 inches in Somerdale (Camden County) and 1.0 inch in Pennsauken (Camden County).The low pressure system responsible for the snow moved from the Central Plains the morning of the 15th, east to Kansas City, Missouri during the early evening of the 15th, moved through the Tennessee Valley during the first half of the day on the 16th and reached West Virginia during the early afternoon of the 16th. A secondary low pressure system then developed on the low's warm front near Norfolk, Virginia and became the primary low pressure system by early in the evening on the 16th when it was just east of Wallops Island, Virginia. It moved quickly offshore and was southeast of Nantucket, Massachusetts by 1 a.m. EST on the 17th.

A slow moving low pressure at both the surface and aloft produced periods of rain across New Jersey from the morning of the 12th into the evening of the 13th. The heaviest bursts of rain fell during the evening of the 12th, before sunrise on the 13th, around Noon EDT on the 13th and during the evening of the 13th. Two day storm totals averaged between 2.0 inches and 4.0 inches with the highest amounts in the southeast part of the state. The heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and ponding of water in fields and in low lying areas during heavier precipitation bursts. Precipitation amounts were lighter in Sussex County. The heavy rain also caused stream and river flooding in Mercer and Somerset Counties.Specific storm totals included 4.09 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 3.61 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.25 inches in Wildwood (Cape May County), 2.74 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 2.70 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County), 2.62 inches in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 2.53 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 2.44 inches in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 2.40 inches in Bound Brook (Somerset County), 2.37 inches in Somerville (Somerset County), 2.34 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.32 inches in Somerdale (Camden County), 2.31 inches in Wrightstown (Burlington County), 2.26 inches in Millville (Cumberland County), 2.13 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 2.05 inches in Chatham (Morris County), 2.00 inches in Columbia (Warren County) and Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.96 inches in Riegelsville (Warren County), 1.95 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.81 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County) and 1.71 inches in Long Valley (Morris County).  The low pressure system responsible for the heavy rain moved from Mississippi the morning of the 12th slowly northeast into Alabama during the evening of the 12th, extreme eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 13th, near Philadelphia early in the evening of the 13th and reached the Berkshires of Massachusetts just after midnight EDT on the 14th. The slow movement permitted this low pressure system to gather moisture from both the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean and dropped it onto the Middle Atlantic States.

The combination of heavy rain and snow melt caused poor drainage flooding and eventually flooding along the Maurice Creek in Cumberland County. Heavier rain fell from the middle of the morning into the middle of the evening on the 6th. Poor drainage flooding in Upper Deerfield Township flooded fields and backyards and at least the basement of one home. The Maurice River at Norma was above its 4 foot flood stage from Noon EST on the 8th through 530 a.m. EST on the 10th. It crested at 4.09 feet at 215 a.m. EST on the 9th.  Storm totals included 2.10 inches in Millville, 1.68 inches in Seabrook and 1.46 inches in Vineland.The low pressure system responsible for the heavy rain developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley) moved through the region during the evening of the 6th and ended the rain. Precipitation initially started as a wintry mix around Midnight EST on the 6th and changed over to plain rain before sunrise on the 6th.

The combination of heavy rain and snow melt caused poor drainage flooding and flooding along the Cooper River in Camden County. Heavier rain fell from the middle of the morning into the middle of the evening on the 6th. New Jersey State Route 38 in Cherry Hill was closed from 1 p.m. EST through the evening commute because of flooding under a railroad bridge near Chapel Avenue. The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 304 p.m. EST on the 6th through 300 a.m. EST on the 7th. It crested at 3.18 feet at 800 p.m. EST on the 6th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley) moved through the region during the evening of the 6th and ended the rain. Precipitation initially started as a wintry mix shortly after Midnight EST on the 6th and changed over to plain rain during the morning of the 6th.

A quick moving occluded front and a low pressure system that developed on it off the Delmarva Peninsula dropped a quick one to four inches across the northern half of New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 8th. Some locally higher accumulations occurred in Morris and Sussex Counties. Most of the accumulations were on grassy surfaces except for extreme northwest New Jersey where some slushy snow accumulated on secondary and tertiary roads. Precipitation began as rain around midnight EST on the 8th. As the low pressure system intensified off the Delmarva Peninsula and precipitation fell heavier, the rain changed to snow in most areas between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST. When the precipitation intensity weakened at the end of the event, in some places the snow changed back to rain before ending by noon EST. Specific accumulations included 4.5 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 4.3 inches in Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 4.0 inches in Milton (Morris County), 3.6 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.3 inches in Budd Lake (Morris County) and Clarksburg (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Chatham (Morris County), Blairstown (Warren County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.7 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in South River (Middlesex County), 1.5 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 1.3 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.2 inches in Pottersville and Belle Mead (Somerset County).An occluded front moved from the Upper Mississippi Valley on the evening of the 6th, to the Great Lakes the morning of the 7th, to central Pennsylvania the evening of the 7th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of this occluded front over the Delmarva coastal waters during the early morning of the 8th. It intensified rapidly, but was also moving offshore quickly. Before it moved far enough offshore, it gave the northern half of New Jersey several hours of accumulating snow.

For the second time in the same work week snow, some of it heavy, fell across central and northern New Jersey.  Across northern New Jersey precipitation started as snow shortly after midnight EST on the 19th. From Mercer County southward, precipitation began as rain during the late evening on the 18th.  As the precipitation intensity increased, the rain changed over to snow between 1230 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 19th. Snow fell heavy at times toward sunrise before it ended during the morning. As precipitation intensity diminished after 7 a.m. EST, the snow mixed with rain again. Accumulations ranged between two and seven inches in most locations. Accumulations were also elevation dependent with the highest amounts in the higher terrain of northwestern New Jersey. Many schools in the northern half of the state had delayed openings. The number of accidents overall were less than what occurred with the snow on the 16th as most of it, including the heaviest snow, fell overnight.  Specific accumulations included 7.0 inches in Marcella (Morris County), Sparta (Sussex County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 6.3 inches in Budd Lake (Morris County), 6.1 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 6.0 inches in Clarksburg (Monmouth County), Hope (Warren County) and Parsippany (Morris County), 4.5 inches in Cream Ridge (Monmouth County), 4.3 inches in Ewing (Mercer County), 3.8 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 3.5 inches in Hamilton Square (Mercer County), 3.1 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 3.0 inches in Browns Mills (Burlington County), Oakhurst (Monmouth County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 2.7 inches in Belle Mead (Somerset County), 2.5 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County) and Hammonton (Atlantic County), 2.4 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.2 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 1.5 inches in Somerdale (Camden County)  The low pressure systems that caused the snow moved from Alberta, Canada and western Texas the morning of the 17th, into Wisconsin and Oklahoma on the morning of the 18th. During the afternoon of the 18th, the southern stream low pressure system had moved into the Ohio Valley and started the process of becoming the main system as the second low weakened in Wisconsin. By 1 a.m. EST on the 19th, a consolidated low pressure system was located in far northwestern Virginia. This low moved east and was over the Delmarva Peninsula coastal waters on the morning of the 19th and then scooted east to about 225 miles east of Atlantic City at 1 p.m. EST on the 19th.

A quick moving occluded front and a low pressure system that developed on it off the Delmarva Peninsula dropped a quick one to four inches across the northern half of New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 8th. Some locally higher accumulations occurred in Morris and Sussex Counties. Most of the accumulations were on grassy surfaces except for extreme northwest New Jersey where some slushy snow accumulated on secondary and tertiary roads. Precipitation began as rain around midnight EST on the 8th. As the low pressure system intensified off the Delmarva Peninsula and precipitation fell heavier, the rain changed to snow in most areas between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST. When the precipitation intensity weakened at the end of the event, in some places the snow changed back to rain before ending by noon EST. Specific accumulations included 4.5 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 4.3 inches in Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 4.0 inches in Milton (Morris County), 3.6 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.3 inches in Budd Lake (Morris County) and Clarksburg (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Chatham (Morris County), Blairstown (Warren County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.7 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in South River (Middlesex County), 1.5 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 1.3 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.2 inches in Pottersville and Belle Mead (Somerset County).An occluded front moved from the Upper Mississippi Valley on the evening of the 6th, to the Great Lakes the morning of the 7th, to central Pennsylvania the evening of the 7th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of this occluded front over the Delmarva coastal waters during the early morning of the 8th. It intensified rapidly, but was also moving offshore quickly. Before it moved far enough offshore, it gave the northern half of New Jersey several hours of accumulating snow.

A late winter storm dropped heavy snow in Northwestern New Jersey, a wintry mix in the Raritan Basin and the rest of the northern half of New Jersey and snow to rain across the eastern Philadelphia suburbs on the 16th. Numerous accidents occurred and traffic was snarled on Interstates 80 and 287 and U.S Routes 1 and 9 in the northern half of the state. Several vehicles overturned on the New Jersey Turnpike and one overturned on Interstate 295 in Burlington County. Most of the accidents were minor and involved vehicles slipping off of roads and into signs and ditches. In the northern half of the state, some schools were closed. Most others dismissed early. Evening classes and government meetings were cancelled. Snow spread from south to north during the morning of the 16th. As warmer air moved in from the Atlantic Ocean, precipitation changed to rain in the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area during the early afternoon of the 16th. Farther north in the Raritan Valley and adjacent parts of northern New Jersey, it took longer for precipitation to change to rain by early evening. Precipitation then changed back to snow later that evening before ending shortly after midnight EST on the 17th. In Northwest New Jersey, precipitation fell mainly as snow, as some sleet occurred during the afternoon and early evening. The snow ended shortly after midnight EST on the 17th. Accumulations ranged from two to eight inches, with the highest amounts in Sussex and Morris Counties.  In Mercer County, serious accidents occurred in five townships. Northbound U.S. Route 1 was closed during the evening in Lawrence Township because of several accidents. In East Windsor Township, a school bus slid into a tree. On the same block, another school bus collided with a car. Nineteen children and two adults suffered cuts and bruises. In Somerset County, Interstate 287 was closed in Bedminster Township because of a serious accident. Several accidents also occurred on Interstate 287 in Bridgewater Township.  Specific accumulations included 7.5 inches in Sparta (Sussex County), 7.0 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County), 6.5 inches in Milton (Morris County), 6.3 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 6.1 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 6.0 inches in Whippany (Morris County), Blairstown (Warren County)  and Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 5.5 inches in Boonton (Morris County) and Newton (Sussex County), 5.0 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), Hackettstown (Warren County) and Metuchen (Middlesex County), 4.5 inches in Manalapan (Monmouth County), 4.3 inches in Somerville (Somerset County), 4.0 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 3.8 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County) and Belle Mead (Somerset County), 3.5 inches in Ewing Township (Mercer County),  New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Manasquan (Monmouth County), 3.1 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.8 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 2.2 inches in Somerdale (Camden County) and 1.0 inch in Pennsauken (Camden County).The low pressure system responsible for the snow moved from the Central Plains the morning of the 15th, east to Kansas City, Missouri during the early evening of the 15th, moved through the Tennessee Valley during the first half of the day on the 16th and reached West Virginia during the early afternoon of the 16th. A secondary low pressure system then developed on the low's warm front near Norfolk, Virginia and became the primary low pressure system by early in the evening on the 16th when it was just east of Wallops Island, Virginia. It moved quickly offshore and was southeast of Nantucket, Massachusetts by 1 a.m. EST on the 17th.

The pressure difference (gradient) between an intensifying low pressure system south of Cape Cod, Massachusetts and a high pressure system over the western Great Lakes produced wind gusts to around 45 mph during the evening of the 4th across eastern New Jersey. The peak wind gusts at both the Atlantic City International and Newark Liberty International Airports were 46 mph.

Gusty northwest winds behind a cold front snapped a pine tree on Goshen-Swaiton Road in Middle Township. One home lost power for one hour. The peak wind gust at the Atlantic City International Airport was 44 mph.

A quick moving occluded front and a low pressure system that developed on it off the Delmarva Peninsula dropped a quick one to four inches across the northern half of New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 8th. Some locally higher accumulations occurred in Morris and Sussex Counties. Most of the accumulations were on grassy surfaces except for extreme northwest New Jersey where some slushy snow accumulated on secondary and tertiary roads. Precipitation began as rain around midnight EST on the 8th. As the low pressure system intensified off the Delmarva Peninsula and precipitation fell heavier, the rain changed to snow in most areas between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST. When the precipitation intensity weakened at the end of the event, in some places the snow changed back to rain before ending by noon EST. Specific accumulations included 4.5 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 4.3 inches in Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 4.0 inches in Milton (Morris County), 3.6 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.3 inches in Budd Lake (Morris County) and Clarksburg (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Chatham (Morris County), Blairstown (Warren County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.7 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in South River (Middlesex County), 1.5 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 1.3 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.2 inches in Pottersville and Belle Mead (Somerset County).An occluded front moved from the Upper Mississippi Valley on the evening of the 6th, to the Great Lakes the morning of the 7th, to central Pennsylvania the evening of the 7th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of this occluded front over the Delmarva coastal waters during the early morning of the 8th. It intensified rapidly, but was also moving offshore quickly. Before it moved far enough offshore, it gave the northern half of New Jersey several hours of accumulating snow.

The combination of heavy rain and snow melt caused poor drainage and creek flooding in Gloucester County. Heavier rain fell from the middle of the morning into the middle of the evening on the 6th. Flooding was reported in Deptford and Monroe Townships with at least one business inundated by flood waters. The Raccoon Creek at Swedesboro was above its 13 foot flood stage from 631 p.m. EST on the 6th through 934 a.m. EST on the 7th. It crested at 14.05 feet at 1230 a.m. EST on the 7th. Storm totals included 1.92 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport. The low pressure system responsible for the heavy rain developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley) moved through the region during the evening of the 6th and ended the rain. Precipitation initially started as a wintry mix during the late evening of the 5th and changed over to plain rain during the morning of the 6th.

The combination of heavy rain and snow melt caused poor drainage and river flooding throughout Somerset County. Heavier rain fell from the middle of the morning into the middle of the evening on the 6th. An ice jam developed on the Green Brook and dynamite was used to break the jam. Before that, water backed to Leyland Avenue in North Plainfield and flooded the basements of six homes. In Franklin Township both the Blackwells Mills and Griggstown Causeways were closed because of flooding along the Millstone River. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 206 p.m. EST on the 6th through 824 a.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 15.31 feet at 1015 a.m. EST on the 7th. Farther downstream at Blackwells Mills, the Millstone River was above its 9 foot flood stage from 8 a.m. EST on the 7th through 353 a.m. EST on the 8th. It crested at 9.52 feet at 514 p.m. EST on the 7th. The North Branch of the Raritan River was above its 10 foot flood stage from 741 p.m. EST on the 6th through 802 a.m. EST on the 7th. It crested at 11.32 feet at 1145 p.m. EST on the 6th. Storm totals included 1.95 inches in Somerville, 1.88 inches in Pottersville, 1.80 inches in Bound Brook and 1.77 inches in Blackwells Mills. The low pressure system responsible for the heavy rain developed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 5th. It moved northeast reaching the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 5th and into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 6th. Its associated warm front moved north from the Carolinas on the morning of the 6th into the lower Delmarva Peninsula during the afternoon of the 6th before stalling. The occluded front associated with the primary low pressure system (It went northeast from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley) moved through the region during the evening of the 6th and ended the rain. Precipitation initially started as a wintry mix before sunrise on the 6th and changed over to plain rain during the morning of the 6th.

As a low pressure system developed rapidly off the Southeast NJ Coast between 1 am and 7 am Friday morning, March 19th, bands of heavy snow occurred across most of Western Passaic County.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 5 to 7 inches.  Here are selected snowfall amounts:West Milford - 7.5 inches.Greenwood Lake - 7.0 inches.Charlottesburg - 5.0 inches.

A weak warm front that moved toward northwest New Jersey during the night of the 20th produced some light freezing rain and pockets of sleet, especially over the higher terrain. Air temperatures in valley locations were generally above freezing and only some sleet occurred at the start of precipitation during the late evening. Untreated roadways became slippery. Ice accretions on exposed surfaces were generally less than five hundredths of an inch. In Lincoln Park (Morris County), the sleet covered the ground during the late evening of the 20th.

A low pressure system that developed over the Southern Plains brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to northwest New Jersey. The worst reported conditions were over the higher terrain of Sussex County where temperatures failed to get above the freezing mark. Precipitation began as snow in all areas between 9 a.m. and 11 a.m. EST. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain by Noon EST and changed over to plain rain during the early afternoon in Warren and Morris Counties and central and southern Sussex County. Precipitation remained freezing rain over the northern and higher terrain locations in Sussex County until it ended during the evening. In Morris and Warren Counties there was little, if any, snow accumulation and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. Across Sussex County, accumulations reached up to 2 inches and ice accretions ranged from one-tenth of an inch in the central and southern part of the county to around one-quarter of an inch in the northern part of the county. Many after school and evening activities were cancelled. Weather related accidents with minor injuries occurred in Morris County.Accumulations included 2 inches in Wantage (Sussex County). The winter weather was caused by a low pressure system that moved from eastern Texas on the morning of the second to Michigan on the morning of the 3rd. A secondary low pressure system formed in North Carolina on the morning of the 3rd and moved north and passed close to New York City early in the evening on the 3rd before moving into Maine early in the morning on the 4th. While the high pressure system position at the onset of precipitation was not in an ideal position to lock in the cold air (It was already over Nova Scotia.), the recent unseasonably cold weather kept sufficient cold air in place near the surface for the wintry mix in northwestern New Jersey.

An offshore low pressure system's western edge of its precipitation shield passed over New Jersey and dropped heavy snow from Cape May County north through Ocean County. Accumulations averaged 4 to 6 inches. Farther to the north or west, there was a sharp drop in snowfall intensity and accumulations were generally 2 inches or less. Snow began falling during the late afternoon. Temperatures remained above or near freezing through the evening commute and the snow did not start accumulating on roadways until after the commute was over. The first reports of accumulating snow were received after 8 p.m. EST. The snow fell heavy at times during the second half of the evening and ended during the early morning of the 18th. No major accidents were reported on either the Garden State Parkway or Atlantic City Expressway. Schools were closed on the 18th in and around Atlantic City. Specific accumulations included 6.5 inches in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 6.0 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 5.5 inches in Egg Harbor City (Atlantic County), 5.0 inches in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), Ship Bottom (Ocean County), Green Creek and Cold Spring (Cape May County), 4.5 inches in Woodbine (Cape May County), 4.1 inches in Cape May City (Cape May County), 3.9 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.8 inches in Port Elizabeth (Cumberland County), 3.7 inches in Beachwood (Ocean County), 2.5 inches in Browns Mills (Burlington County) and Manasquan (Monmouth County), 2.3 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 2.0 inches in Oakhurst (Monmouth County) and 0.8 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County). The low pressure system responsible for the snow developed along the coastal waters east of Georgia during the morning of the 17th. By the early evening of the 17th, a pair of low pressure systems were located east of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina and Charleston, South Carolina. These combined into one overnight and passed about 250 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast on the morning of the 18th. The low was located too far offshore to back the heavy snow bands any farther west than the southeastern New Jersey coastal counties.

A trained SKYWARN Spotter measured a wind gust to 59 mph during a severe thunderstorm in Franklin Township.
A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous large tree limbs in the southwestern part of Sussex County affecting Hampton and Stillwater Townships the most.
A severe thunderstorm tore down several poles and wires in Woodbridge.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous limbs, several trees and wires across northeastern Gloucester County in Paulsboro Borough, Woodbury City, West Deptford and Washington Townships. In addition to the winds, pea to marble size hail also fell during the severe thunderstorm.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large tree limbs and small trees in Tabernacle Township. A SKYWARN Spotter and an observer at McGuire Air Force Base both measured wind gusts to 56 mph.

Lightning struck six homes in Washington Township. The worst damage occurred to a house on Chapel Heights Road. Lightning struck the top of the chimney, traveled down it and ignited a fire. Fire damage occurred to the chimney, roof, outside and one inside wall of the house. Smoke damage occurred elsewhere in the house. Other homes were struck by lightning on Delsea Drive, Fries Mill Road, Melody Lane, Walton Drive and Pitman Downer Road. No serious damage was reported from the other homes. No serious injuries were reported.
A lightning strike started a fire at the Readington Middle School in Readington Township. No major damage was reported.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and blew over a fence in South Plainfield Borough. There were also numerous reports of quarter size hail. In neighboring Edison Township the same thunderstorm tore down wires on three blocks. The combination of the damaging winds and lightning caused about 20,000 Public Service Electric and Gas customers to lose power throughout the day in central New Jersey including Middlesex County.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down large tree limbs and wires in Delaware Township.
In addition to the large hail, wind gusts to around 40 mph accompanied the thunderstorm in Princeton.

The combination of spring tides caused by the full moon and a residual onshore flow caused by a departing low pressure system caused minor tidal flooding during the late afternoon and early evening high tide cycle along coastal New Jersey. The high tide reached 6.9 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.8 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding usually starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. The low pressure system which caused the onshore flow formed along a warm frontal boundary on the Delmarva Peninsula during the evening of May 31st and moved slowly northeast on June 1st. The low pressure system passed closed to Nantucket, Massachusetts during the afternoon of June 1st.

A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in and around the Salem Nuclear Power Plant in Lower Alloways Creek Township. Sheet metal was torn from one building, a trailer was flipped, scaffolding was blown apart and decorative windows were blown out.  Walnut size hail also broke windshields and damaged vehicles. Numerous trees were knocked down in the township.

A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in and around the Salem Nuclear Power Plant in Lower Alloways Creek Township. Sheet metal was torn from one building, a trailer was flipped, scaffolding was blown apart and decorative windows were blown out.  Walnut size hail also broke windshields and damaged vehicles. Numerous trees were knocked down in the township.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down large tree limbs and wires in the Blackwood area of Gloucester Township.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires in Salem City.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down tree limbs and wires in Lower Alloways Creek Township.

As a severe thunderstorm moved east across Passaic County, it produced high winds that downed a tree on Route 23 in West Milford, high tension wires in Wayne, and tree limbs in Pompton Lakes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a severe thunderstorm moved slowly east, it produced large hail.  The broadcast media reported golfball size hail in Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm pulled down a tree in Paulsboro and large tree limbs and wires in Woodbury.
Large tree limbs and wires were pulled down by a severe thunderstorm in Lumberton Township.

A severe thunderstorm pulled down large tree limbs and wires in Lumberton Township. A SKYWARN Spotter in the township had a measured wind gust of 56 mph and also detected a wall cloud with the thunderstorm.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down several small trees and large limbs in New Hanover Township.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and numerous limbs in extreme southwestern Monmouth County in Allentown Borough and Upper Freehold Township. A SKYWARN Spotter reported a measured wind gust to 52 mph in Cream Ridge.

A severe thunderstorm pulled down large tree limbs and wires in the Nesco area of Mullica Township.

A funnel cloud formed from a thunderstorm as it moved across High Point State Park in Montague Township. The funnel crossed New Jersey State Park 23 before it dissipated. No wind damage occurred as it did not touch down. The same thunderstorm did drop small hail (pea to marble size) over the township. The hail was so heavy that the highway department had to salt the roads around and leading into High Point State Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a severe thunderstorm moved east, it produced high winds that downed numerous trees and wires in Kearny and on Route 507.

As heavy thunderstorms moved slowly east across Western Union County, they produced torrential rain.  Flash flooding resulted in the closure of numerous roads in Plainfield and along Route 22 in Scotch Plains.  Spotters also reported basement flooding in Cranford and street flooding in Westfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Watchung Borough. The same thunderstorm dropped small hail (pea to marble size) around the interchange of Interstates 78 and 287 in Bridgewater Township. The hail was heavy enough to briefly cover the roadway.

The same thunderstorm that produced wind damage also produced torrential downpours and caused flooding of small streams and yards in High Bridge Borough. Doppler Radar Storm Total estimates reached around two inches, most of which fell within one hour.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Watchung Borough. The same thunderstorm dropped small hail (pea to marble size) around the interchange of Interstates 78 and 287 in Bridgewater Township. The hail was heavy enough to briefly cover the roadway.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down large tree limbs and wires in Carteret Borough.
A severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of trees in Bedminster Township. One tree fell on a vehicle, but the occupants were not injured.
Several trees were knocked down by a severe thunderstorm in Lambertville City.
A severe thunderstorm coupled with heavy rain caused an apartment wall in Camden to collapse. The same thunderstorm also knocked down a few trees and wires in and around Camden.

Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours caused flooding in and around Woodbridge Township. Rapidly rising waters caused the closures of sections of U.S. Routes 1 and 9, Green Street and Amboy Avenue. Nearly a dozen vehicles became disabled because of the flood waters and one woman was rescued from her car on U.S. Route 9. Doppler Radar Storm Total estimates reached 2 to 3 inches in Woodbridge. A SKYWARN Spotter in Avenel (Woodbridge Township) reported a storm total of 2.65 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a series of severe thunderstorms moved across Bergen County, they produced large hail and high winds.  A trained spotter reported nickel size hail in Oakland.  Law enforcement reported downed power lines in North Arlington.
As a severe thunderstorm moved across the area, it produced penny size hail in Lyndhurst, as reported by a spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and wires in Clinton Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a heavy thunderstorm moved across the region, it produced torrential rain.  Flash flooding resulted in the closure of Routes 1 and 9 near Newark International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm pulled down several trees and wires along the Clinton and Tewksbury Township border.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down one tree and several large tree limbs and wires in Mullica Hill (Harrison Township).
Lightning struck a Public Service Electric and Gas substation in Edison and caused 4,700 homes and businesses to lose power.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and blew over a fence in South Plainfield Borough. There were also numerous reports of quarter size hail. In neighboring Edison Township the same thunderstorm tore down wires on three blocks. The combination of the damaging winds and lightning caused about 20,000 Public Service Electric and Gas customers to lose power throughout the day in central New Jersey including Middlesex County.

Thunderstorms also produced torrential downpours in and around Mullica Hill in Harrison Township. This produced poor drainage flooding and flooding of streams in the township. Numerous roads and lawns were flooded. Doppler Radar Storm Total estimates reached between 2 and 3 inches, most of which fell within one hour.
Nickel size hail fell over the Bamboo Brook Outdoor Education Center in Chester Township.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down several large tree limbs and wires in Far Hills Borough.

A cluster of thunderstorms produced considerable cloud-to-ground lightning across northwestern New Jersey. Approximately 5,200 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in northeast Pennsylvania and northwest New Jersey between 11 p.m. EDT on the 10th and 1 a.m. EDT on the 11th. About 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light Customers lost power in northwestern New Jersey. Concentrated outages occurred in Dover and Boonton in Morris County and Newton in Sussex County. Nine other municipalities in Sussex County and Blairstown in Warren County also reported power outages. In Parsippany (Morris County), the sixth green of the Knoll West Country Club was scorched brown in three distinct patterns after being struck by lightning.

Lightning struck a church in Neptune. No significant damage was reported. Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning caused about 7,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers to lose power in central New Jersey. All power was restored in Monmouth County by the late evening on the 13th.

Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning caused about 7,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers to lose power in central New Jersey. In Ocean County, hardest hit were Dover and Brick Townships. Power was not completely restored until 2 a.m. EDT on the 14th in Toms River.
Lightning struck the top of the chimney at McKinny Hall on College Avenue at Rutgers University. Bricks loosened and fell to the ground.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and power lines in North Plainfield. About 20,000 Public Service Electric and Gas customers lost power because of the damaging winds and lightning in central New Jersey.
For the second time in the same evening, a severe thunderstorm dropped large hail (this time quarter size) in Hillsborough Township.
SKYWARN Spotters reported penny size hail in both Somerville Borough and Hillsborough Township.
Half dollar size hail fell in Kingwood Township and pea size hail fell in Readington Township.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Fredon Township. The combination of frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and winds caused power outages to about 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light Customers in northwestern New Jersey.

A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Blairstown Township. The combination of frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and winds caused power outages to about 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light Customers in northwestern New Jersey.

A cluster of thunderstorms produced considerable cloud-to-ground lightning across northwestern New Jersey. Approximately 5,200 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in northeast Pennsylvania and northwest New Jersey between 11 p.m. EDT on the 10th and 1 a.m. EDT on the 11th. About 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light Customers lost power in northwestern New Jersey. Concentrated outages occurred in Dover and Boonton in Morris County and Newton in Sussex County. Nine other municipalities in Sussex County and Blairstown in Warren County also reported power outages. In Parsippany (Morris County), the sixth green of the Knoll West Country Club was scorched brown in three distinct patterns after being struck by lightning.
Hail as large as quarters fell in both North and South Brunswick Townships. The hail fell for up to ten minutes. Half inch diameter hail also fell in Monroe Township.
Hail as large as quarters fell in both North and South Brunswick Townships. The hail fell for up to ten minutes. Half inch diameter hail also fell in Monroe Township.

A 9-year-old and 6-year-old brother and sister were injured by lightning while they stood next to each other and their father on a tennis court in Mercer County Park. Lightning apparently struck an adjacent tennis court and then struck the boy's left leg and knocked a shoe off his foot and sent it flying. His 6-year-old sister fell backward and struck her head on the ground. She developed a large lump. The boy was unable to walk and complained about pain in his lower leg and foot.

A cluster of thunderstorms produced considerable cloud-to-ground lightning across northwestern New Jersey. Approximately 5,200 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in northeast Pennsylvania and northwest New Jersey between 11 p.m. EDT on the 10th and 1 a.m. EDT on the 11th. About 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light Customers lost power in northwestern New Jersey. Concentrated outages occurred in Dover and Boonton in Morris County and Newton in Sussex County. Nine other municipalities in Sussex County and Blairstown in Warren County also reported power outages. In Parsippany (Morris County), the sixth green of the Knoll West Country Club was scorched brown in three distinct patterns after being struck by lightning.
Penny size hail fell in Frankford Township and Branchville Borough.
A dust devil was spotted by a pilot as it moved across a field in Readington Township. There was no property damage.

A cold front approached from the west on this day. A moderately moist airmass preceded this front. Thunderstorms developed well ahead of the front and produced heavy rain and a few severe weather reports...mainly hail.

An unusually strong upper low pressure system moved slowly east across the Great Lakes States on this day. A strong jet stream was associated with this system. At the same time...a warm front approached from the southwest. This scenario set the stage for the development of severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. The worst storm of the day was a supercell that moved southeastward through Bergen County...producing a funnel cloud and golfball size hail along it's path.

Several lightning strikes injured two men and started a few fires in Brick Township. A 48-year-old man and a 40-year-old man were injured when they were struck by lightning while standing under a tree on Windward Beach. Lightning apparently struck the tree first and it exploded into pieces. It then entered the 40-year-old man through his head (quarter size singe mark) and exited through his feet and then entered the 48-year-old man through his head and exited through his feet. Both men were treated and released from the hospital. A lightning strike ignited a roof fire of a Lake Shore Drive home. Another lightning strike and ensuing fire burned a shed behind another home to the ground. Lightning struck a Cambridge Way home and made a two foot diameter hole in the roof, but no fire occurred.
In addition to the lightning and the hail, the thunderstorms pulled down large tree limbs and wires in both Lakewood and Brick Townships.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down wires and large tree limbs in Frenchtown Borough.
Nickel size hail fell in Lakewood.
Nickel size hail also fell in Brick Township.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down power lines and large tree limbs in Manasquan Borough.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and blocked a lane on the Garden State Parkway.
Flash flooding of streets resulted in water rescues.
A wind gust of 50 knots (58 mph) was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 17th featured a nearly stationary warm front that was draped just north of the Tri-State area. The air just south of this front was very moist...with dewpoints in the 70s. During the afternoon...thunderstorms developed near this warm front and moved southeast at a slow rate of speed. These storms passed over portions of Northeast New Jersey...New York City and Long Island and produced torrential downpours. This caused flash flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain reported in 30 minutes due to thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires from Pennington in Hopewell Township east through Princeton Township.

A very moist airmass...with dewpoints of 70 to 75 degrees...preceded a cold front that crossed through the tri-state area on the 23rd of July. This set the stage for the development of thunderstorms with torrential rainfall across Northeast New Jersey. The thunderstorms were slow-moving...producing rainfall rates of up to 2.5" per hour which caused flash flooding.

A funnel cloud formed from a thunderstorm as it moved across High Point State Park in Montague Township. The funnel crossed New Jersey State Park 23 before it dissipated. No wind damage occurred as it did not touch down. The same thunderstorm did drop small hail (pea to marble size) over the township. The hail was so heavy that the highway department had to salt the roads around and leading into High Point State Park.
As a severe thunderstorm moved east across Passaic County, it produced high winds that downed a tree on Route 23 in West Milford, high tension wires in Wayne, and tree limbs in Pompton Lakes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a severe thunderstorm moved northeast across Passaic County, it produced large hail and torrential rain.  Amateur radio operators reported nickel size hail in Pompton Lakes and flash flooding along Hamburg Turnpike at Glenwild Ave. in Bloomingdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of severe thunderstorms knocked down numerous trees and wires, dropped large hail across the western part of the county and produced two funnel clouds over Vineland and Port Elizabeth. The funnel clouds did not reach the ground. Nevertheless, SKYWARN Spotters measured wind gusts to 68 mph in Port Elizabeth (Maurice River Township), 62 mph in Newport and 60 mph in Fortescue (both in Downe Township). About 6,200 homes and businesses lost power in the county, with the greatest concentration in Greenwich Township. Power was restored to all except 900 in the Port Norris (Commercial Township) area on the 15th. Downed trees and wires were reported in the following municipalities: Bridgeton, Commercial, Downe, Greenwich, Lawrence, Maurice River and Millville. Several roads were blocked by the downed trees and one tree fell through the windows of a home in Bridgeton City. Large hail (as large as quarter size) also fell in Greenwich, Deerfield and Upper Deerfield Townships.

Heavy surf from swells generated by Hurricane Alex pounded the New Jersey coast, especially on the 3rd. At least four swimmers in Cape May and one swimmer in Atlantic City were hospitalized by injuries caused by the surf.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large trees in Hillsborough Township along Amwell Road near its intersection with U.S. Route 206. A few other trees were snapped and aluminum siding was torn from a couple of homes in the Majestic Knolls section of the township. Several large mats were tossed in the air. Power lines were knocked down in Hillsborough and also in Millstone Borough. About 120 homes and businesses lost power in the two municipalities and power was restored in about 90 minutes.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Mendham Borough, including across New Jersey State Route 24. A Skywarn Spotter also reported quarter size hail with the severe thunderstorm. Severe thunderstorms caused about 700 homes and businesses to lose power because of downed trees and wires during the afternoon of the 11th in Morris County. All power was restored by the evening.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Mendham Borough, including across New Jersey State Route 24. A Skywarn Spotter also reported quarter size hail with the severe thunderstorm. Severe thunderstorms caused about 700 homes and businesses to lose power because of downed trees and wires during the afternoon of the 11th in Morris County. All power was restored by the evening.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Madison Borough. Severe thunderstorms caused about 700 homes and businesses to lose power because of downed trees and wires during the afternoon of the 11th in Morris County. All power was restored by the evening.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down whole trees in Bedminster Township. About 660 homes and businesses lost power in the county because of downed trees and wires during the afternoon and early evening.
A Skywarn Spotter reported penny size hail fell in White House Station (Readington Township).

Three golfers were injured at the Heron Glen Country Club in Ringoes (East Amwell Township) when lightning struck a tree while they were passing under it with a golf cart. The tree splintered and limbs and debris fell on them. A 55-year-old man, 53-year-old man and 51-year-old woman were briefly  hospitalized with cuts and bruises.

A series of thunderstorms with very heavy rain between 1 p.m. and 6 p.m. EDT caused poor drainage flooding, stream flooding in the northern part of the county and flooding along the Assunpink Creek in Mercer County. The Assunpink Creek at Trenton was above its 7 foot flood stage from 635 p.m. EDT through 907 p.m. EDT on the 23rd. It crested at 7.05 feet at 715 p.m. EDT. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 4 and 5 inches in sections of Hopewell Township. Storm totals included 1.83 inches in Trenton and 1.53 inches in Lawrenceville.

Several thunderstorms that moved along the Ocean and Monmouth County border produced flash flooding in Howell Township (Monmouth County) and Lakewood and Brick Townships (Ocean County). In Brick Township, several roads were closed. A few people were trapped in vehicles and were rescued. One vehicle was nearly submerged on Clay Circle. A few roads were also closed in Howell Township. Flooding caused traffic delays along the Garden State Parkway between mile markers 91 and 92. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded five inches along the county and township borders. A SKYWARN Spotter in Brick Township recorded 2.25 inches of rain.

In Brick Township, several roads were closed. A few people were trapped in vehicles and were rescued. One vehicle was nearly submerged on Clay Circle. A few roads were also closed in Howell Township. Flooding caused traffic delays along the Garden State Parkway between mile markers 91 and 92. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded five inches along the county and township borders. A SKYWARN Spotter in Brick Township recorded 2.25 inches of rain.
Nickel size hail fell in Hamilton Township.
The remnants of Hurricane Jeane produced between 3 and 6  inches of rain across Northeast New Jersey. This resulted in widespread flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads closed due to extensive flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains flooded creeks which in tuen flooded roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Countywide flash flooding of streets and highways occuring in Essex County. Road closures resulted, with the Garden State Parkway having several lanes closed.
